tables.title: Post-ROSC Management Targets
rows: Norepinephrine 0.1–0.5 mcg/kg/min, IV fluid bolus 250–500 mL

tables.title: Multimodal Neuroprognostication After Cardiac Arrest
rows: Bilateral absent pupillary + corneal reflexes; absent motor response or extensor posturing
Bilateral absence of cortical N20 response
High specificity; less affected by sedation
Within first days, interpreted at ≥72 h
Status myoclonus, burst suppression without epileptiform discharges, malignant patterns
Must be interpreted in multimodal context; single value not sufficient
Less specific than NSE; adjunctive marker
Diffuse cerebral edema, loss of gray-white differentiation
Early CT may be normal; CT at 48–72 h more informative
Restricted diffusion in cortical and deep gray matter
Most sensitive imaging modality for hypoxic-ischemic injury

tables.title: ECPR: Indications, Contraindications, and Key Parameters
rows: Refractory cardiac arrest (persistent VF/pVT despite standard ACLS: high-quality CPR, ≥3 shocks, epinephrine, antiarrhythmic)
<60 minutes from CPR initiation to ECMO flow
Required — acute MI, PE, myocarditis, drug toxicity, hypothermia, etc.
Consider age, comorbidities, pre-arrest functional status; no absolute contraindications
Femoral: venous drainage (femoral vein) + arterial return (femoral artery), percutaneous under US guidance
Distal perfusion catheter recommended to prevent limb ischemia
Limb ischemia, bleeding, cannula malposition, hemolysis, infection
Bridge to further therapy (PCI, TTM, recovery) — NOT destination therapy
Guided by echocardiographic recovery, hemodynamic stability on decreasing flow, clinical assessment
Experienced, high-volume team with dedicated ECPR infrastructure
